author: @kadmow
text:
Never stop innovating - Imagineering - Rob did that so well - we all walk on the shoulders of giants...

Nothing missed in that analyses- so many options -  not only could this be an engine or  pump - but connect 2 together (or simply have a fixed crank, scotch yoke or ratcheting drive and we have a variable torque / variable speed CVT  (double variable variable transmission - allows even better input and output dynamic matching..  6 minutes thinking with an epicyclic stage and the whole thing could be  a compact reversible rotary CVT - I'm sure (balance is often the issue in real / high speed systems)..

author: @Jay-mq6jh
text:
This is the first video I’ve been recommended since learning of Robert’s passing…………breaks my heart….
Quite arguably, the most intelligent person I’ve had the pleasure of learning from.
I hope that you found peace Sir.
Thanks for everything.
❤

author: @rjung_ch
text: Changing the travel of the piston will increase or decrease the compression, so you could also use this for engines that have different fuels maybe? Cheers 👍💪✌

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: Awesome 🎉

author: @ericsumma7654
text:
I see a complication: the variable piston stroke is referenced to half of the cylinder volume. As the stroke begins to increase the piston draws a minimal amount of fresh air to add to (nearly) half a cylinder of stale air from the previous power stroke. This also means there is a minimal amount of compression. 
  So the efficiency variables include, the changing compression ratio, changing amounts of unburned fresh air and preburned air, as well as the new and preburned fuel.
  It would be interesting to see how they solved these issues. Wow, that looks like a really complicated combustion cycle. 🤔

author: @Robert-y2k7s
text: Greeting to the Dread Mad Scientist Robert! I don’t watch your YouTubes as much as I used to. My bad. But every time I do I marvel at how your construction skills, whether it is with welding, or 3D printing, have improved over the years. Patty, I’m sure watches with pride, I am sure.

author: @davidvwilliamson
text: i made a variable-throw crank once out of steel. It wasn't anywhere near as clever as yours. After many years the idea evolved into a bolt-cutter which I still have- I used it the other day as a jack to repair a broken bookcase. As the americans say 'go figure'

text: This is used in steam trains.   Replace the fly wheel with driving wheel of the loco.

author: @danielfeyerabend904
text:
Hello, Good Doctor!  That's a fascinating mechanism.   I'm looking for a variable stroke length section for my contraption.   This may be it!!  
The 3d printed version wouldn't likely handle the forces I'm applying.   Is there a catalog or source for already available ones made of metal?  
I plan to introduce you to my contraption, hopefully before we get too far into October.

text: A variable stroke piston that has a constant TDC position but a differing BDC would be sweet.

author: @slmason7
text: I remember as a kid (70s) my grandfather experimenting with a variable compression ratio piston design using a cantilever mechanism. I always wonder why you'd want variable compression, now I know. Thank you very much!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That's interesting, and cheers for the comment!

author: @NovaAthens
text: Mazda loves their weird engines don't they.

author: @wanglydiaplt
text: Sure looks like Stephenson! 🙂

author: @corpsie666
text: Imagine how easy it would be for a starter motor to start and engine with that technology. The starter could accelerate to its top RPM with the crank at zero displacement. Then the crank displacement could be increased slowly or quickly or in-between to get things going.

author: @timw1636
text: The simplest brilliant way to simulate variable displacement was done (of course) by the Japanese in the prius. Leaving the intake valve open after intake  TDC and a blow back chamber. A variable cam gear is controlled by oil pressure. It's soo beautiful. Lol

replies.author: @corpsie666
replies.text: You need to do some research before giving them a verbal handj0b regarding LIVC. Also, that wasn't variable displacement, it was variable displacement and variable effective compression ratio.

author: @deanverheul7799
text: Cheers 🍻

author: @Nanna-hpi
text:
I miss the good old days when you fiddled with tin cans and simple stuff, making magics out of them. 
Then I had a chance of making my own experiments. 
Sadly the 3D printer came and I am reduced to dreaming of Legos and Plus Pluses…
I really miss your crazy prehistoric inventions, Bob.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: To be honest mate - you have to move ahead or it just gets stale - you will have to get yourself a 3d printer there are some good ones that are pretty cheap - in fact cheaper than some of the stuff i used to do

replies.author: @Nanna-hpi
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Not where I live. It’s a small island lost in the Indian Ocean where everything is an arm, a leg and a kidney too…
But I understand your point of view and I never wanted to cast a shadow over your joy which is quite infectious 😁
